questions.question: Design an end-to-end, automated hardening and isolation plan for a GCP multi-tenant SaaS (Cloud Run frontend, Cloud SQL Postgres, BigQuery, Pub/Sub). The plan must enforce tenant isolation at runtime and scale to thousands of tenants. Include: per-tenant IAM Roles and Row-Level Security, workload identity federation for DB access, VPC Service Controls with Private Google Access, encryption key lifecycle (CMEK), and an automated synthetic-tenant on-boarding + rollback and validation workflow?
questions.answer: Implement per-tenant schemas with PostgreSQL Row-Level Security, binding database access to per-tenant service accounts via Workload Identity Federation. Enforce access through IAM Conditions on Cloud SQL proxy connections and VPC Service Controls with Private Google Access. Utilize CMEK for per-tenant encryption key lifecycle and deploy an automated synthetic tenant onboarding workflow with validation and rollback capabilities.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates the ability to design a comprehensive, scalable tenant isolation strategy that integrates database security, identity management, network controls, and encryption key management with automated testing and rollback processes essential for production environments.

## Key Concepts

- Row-Level Security in PostgreSQL
- Workload Identity Federation
- IAM Conditions
- Cloud SQL Proxies
- VPC Service Controls and Private Service Connect
- CMEK per-tenant key lifecycle
- Synthetic tenants and rollback workflows

## Code Example

```javascript
// Example per-tenant access control implementation
const tenantAccess = {
  schema: `tenant_${tenantId}`,
  rlsPolicy: `CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON ${table} 
    USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant_id')::uuid)`,
  workloadIdentity: `projects/${project}/locations/${location}/workloadIdentityPools/${pool}/providers/${provider}`,
  cmekKey: `projects/${project}/locations/${location}/keyRings/${keyRing}/cryptoKeys/${tenantId}-key`
};
```
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Tenant[Tenant] --> Identity[Workload Identity Federation]
  Identity --> DB[Cloud SQL Postgres]
  DB --> Network[VPC Service Controls/Private Service Connect]
  Tenant --> Data[BigQuery/Pub/Sub]

What concrete steps and configurations would you implement?
questions.answer: Per-tenant log redaction via Cloud DLP; per-tenant log sinks to separate buckets with IAM Conditions and VPC Service Controls; enable CMEK on sinks; export logs over Private Service Connect to reduce

- Tenant isolation: dataset IAM, RLS/Views, and per-tenant identities

- Egress controls: per-tenant Pub/Sub topics and encryption at rest

-- BigQuery Row-Level Security example (conceptual)
CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY tenant_row_policy
ON `project.dataset.table`
AS

```

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Assess ability to design strict, scalable tenant isolation in a single GCP project using per-tenant datasets and models, with secure onboarding and containment.

## Key Concepts

- Per-tenant data isolation with BigQuery datasets and Cloud Storage prefixes
- CMEK granularity per dataset
- IAM Conditions and Workload Identity Federation
- Vertex AI across tenants and private endpoints
- VPC Service Controls and Private Service Connect
- Containment, rollback, and synthetic tenant testing

## Code Example

```terraform
# Per-tenant BigQuery dataset with CMEK
resource "google_kms_key_ring" "tenant_ring" {
  name     = "tenant-${tenant_id}-ring"
  location = "global"
}
resource "google_kms_crypto_key" "tenant_key" {
  name      = "tenant_${tenant_id}-key"
  key_ring  = google_kms_key_ring.tenant_ring.id
  rotation_period = "31536000s"
}
resource "google_bigquery_dataset" "tenant_ds" {
  dataset_id = "tenant_${tenant_id}"
  location   = "US"
  encryption_config {
    kms_key_name = google_kms_crypto_key.tenant_key.id
  }
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you monitor for cross-tenant data access violations and automate revocation?
- What are the trade-offs of tenant-per-dataset vs. row-level security at scale?
questions.diagram:

## Code Example

```yaml
# Kubernetes RBAC and Namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: tenant-a
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: tenant-a-view
  namespace: tenant-a
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: view
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: "system:serviceaccount:tenant-a:dev-sa"
```

```bash
# IAM conditional binding example (pseudo)
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding my-project \
  --member="serviceAccount:tenant-a-dev@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com" \
  --role="roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser" \
  --condition="expression=resource.name.startsWith('projects/my-project/tenants/tenant-a') && request.time < timestamp '2026-12-31T23:59:59Z'" 
```

Tests ability to design end-to-end tenant isolation in a real-world ML platform, considering data-plane and control-plane boundaries, key management, and automated containment for misconfigurations.

## Key Concepts
- Per-tenant namespaces and projects
- Data plane isolation with CMKs and RLS
- Private connectivity and service perimeter controls
- IAM conditions and least-privilege bindings
- Automated containment and rollback

## Code Example
```json
{
  "bindings": [
    {
      "role": "roles/aiplatform.user",
      "members": ["user:tenantA@example.com"],
      "condition": {
        "title": "TenantIsolatedAccess",
        "expression": "resource.name.startsWith(\"projects/tenantA-\")"
      }
    }
  ],
  "policyVersion": 3
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test cross-tenant leakage scenarios before production?
- What metrics would you surface to detect anomalous cross-tenant access?

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted multi-tenant SaaS with Cloud Run as API, Cloud SQL data, and Storage, all tenants share a single project. A malicious tenant could impersonate another tenant's service account via Workload Identity Federation misconfig. Draft a defense-in-depth plan to prevent this: 1) per-tenant WIF bindings with distinct providers and audience; 2) IAM Conditions and DenyPolicies to block cross-tenant impersonation; 3) token-use monitoring with Cloud Audit/Logging and automated revocation; 4) a CI gate that creates synthetic tenants and tests cross-tenant impersonation with rollback?
questions.answer: Plan emphasizes per-tenant WIF bindings with unique OIDC providers and audiences, tenant-scoped IAM Conditions on impersonation, DenyPolicies to block atypical cross-tenant origins, and automated revo
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of identity boundaries in a shared GCP project, focusing on Workload Identity Federation misconfig risks and practical mitigations.

## Key Concepts
- Workload Identity Federation per-tenant bindings
- IAM Conditions and DenyPolicies for strict impersonation controls
- Cloud Audit Logs for real-time impersonation alerts
- CI gates simulating synthetic tenants and rollback safety

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudo policy illustrating tenant-bound impersonation guardrails
const policy = {
  bindings: [
    {
      role: 'roles/iam.serviceAccountUser',
      members: ['serviceAccount:tenant-*@[PROJECT].iam.gserviceaccount.com'],
      condition: {
        title: 'TenantImpersonationOnly',
        description: 'Impersonation allowed only from tenant-specific WIF provider',
        expression: "request.auth.claims.tenant_id == resource.name.split('/')[1]"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test impersonation guards in a production-like CI environment?
- Which logs and metrics would you alert on to detect evasion attempts?

questions.answer: Assign a unique service account per tenant and grant only to their Cloud Storage prefix or bucket; enable Uniform bucket-level access and avoid object ACLs; use tenant-namespaced paths (tenant-1/…) wi

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Probes a practical per-session access model across orgs using IAM Conditions, Workload Identity Federation, and ACM with strict revocation and auditing.

## Key Concepts

- IAM Conditions
- Workload Identity Federation
- Access Context Manager
- Time-bounded access
- Revocation & auditing
- Synthetic data validation

## Code Example

```javascript
{
  "bindings": [
    {
      "role": "roles/bigquery.jobUser",
      "members": ["serviceAccount:partner-sa@provider-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"],
      "conditions": [
        {
          "title": "TimeWindow",
          "description": "2h access",
          "expression": "request.time >= timestamp('2026-01-13T08:00:00Z') && request.time < timestamp('2026-01-13T10:00:00Z')"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would token leakage be detected and promptly remediated?
- What failure modes exist if the time window misconfigures and how to mitigate?

questions.question: In a multi-tenant GCP SaaS where tenants share Cloud Run deployments and Cloud SQL, design a defense-in-depth plan to guard container images against supply-chain and runtime compromise. How would you implement per-tenant image provenance using Binary Authorization with attestations, tenant-scoped signing keys in CI/CD, per-tenant IAM/Conditions, per-tenant VPC Service Controls restrictions, automated rollback to last-good Cloud Run revision, and synthetic validation tests?
questions.answer: Enforce image provenance with Binary Authorization and per-tenant attestations. Require signed images by per-tenant keys in CI/CD (Artifact Registry) before deployment to Cloud Run. Bind attestations

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question probes practical, scalable isolation in a shared GCP data science stack, focusing on real IAM Conditions, VPC Service Controls, and CMK governance. It also tests how to validate and roll back changes in CI.

## Key Concepts
- IAM Conditions and Data Catalog tagging for data scoping
- Per-brand VPC Service Controls and CMEK
- Vertex AI Workbench isolation and per-brand datasets
- CI validation with synthetic tenants and rollback protocols

## Code Example
```javascript
// Example: IAM binding with a per-brand condition (illustrative)
{
  bindings: [
    {
      role: 'roles/bigquery.dataViewer',
      members: ['serviceAccount:brand-a-sa@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com'],
      condition: {
        title: 'BrandA-only',
        description: 'Restrict to BrandA datasets',
        expression: "resource.name.startsWith(\"projects/PROJECT/datasets/BrandA_\") && resource.data.tags.brand == 'BrandA'"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you automate policy reconciliation when new brands are onboarded?
- What monitoring indicators would you add to detect cross-brand access attempts?

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted SaaS using Cloud Run and Pub/Sub with a single project, tenants share a namespace but should never access each other’s topics and subscriptions. Design a beginner-friendly plan to enforce tenant isolation at runtime via IAM, Pub/Sub resource names, and CI tests. Include: 1) per-tenant service accounts and least privilege; 2) resource-based access controls or topic/subscription naming conventions; 3) synthetic tenant access tests in CI; 4) rollback strategies if cross-tenant access is detected?
questions.answer: Implement tenant isolation in GCP by creating per-tenant service accounts for Cloud Run with least-privilege access. Structure Pub/Sub resources with consistent naming conventions: topics as `topics/tenant-<id>-events` and subscriptions as `subscriptions/tenant-<id>-events`. Grant each service account Publish permission only on their specific topic and Subscribe permission only on their corresponding subscription. Use IAM conditions to enforce tenant ID-based restrictions. In CI, implement synthetic tests that verify each tenant can only access their designated resources and fail fast if cross-tenant access is detected. For rollback procedures, maintain deny policies as safeguards and automated scripts that immediately revoke any misconfigured permissions when cross-tenant access is identified.
questions.explanation:

## Code Example

// Pseudo snippet: IAM bindings for tenant service account
gcp.pubsub.topics["tenant-<id>-events"].addRole(
  "roles/pubsub.publisher", 
  "serviceAccount:tenant-<id>-sa@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
);
```

## Follow-up Questions

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted multi-tenant SaaS on GKE with Cloud SQL PostgreSQL and Pub/Sub, design a defense-in-depth plan to prevent lateral movement and data exfiltration if a tenant namespace is compromised. Include per-tenant Kubernetes RBAC and Pod Security Standards, per-tenant Workload Identity bindings with least privilege, per-tenant CMEK in Cloud KMS with envelope encryption, per-tenant Private Service Connect endpoints and VPC Service Controls, runtime monitoring with Cloud IDS and Cloud Logging, and a CI gate that provisions synthetic tenants and validates cross-tenant access with rollback?
questions.answer: Implement per-tenant Kubernetes RBAC and Pod Security Standards; map tenants to separate Workload Identity bindings with least privileges; encrypt tenant data with per-tenant CMEK via Cloud KMS and envelope encryption; isolate network access with per-tenant Private Service Connect endpoints and VPC Service Controls; monitor runtime with Cloud IDS and Cloud Logging; and validate cross-tenant isolation through CI gates that provision synthetic tenants and automatically rollback on security violations.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Evaluates practical security isolation in a GKE-based multi-tenant SaaS, focusing on Kubernetes security primitives, IAM bindings, data encryption with CMEK, network segmentation, and automated validation.

## Key Concepts

- Kubernetes RBAC and Pod Security Standards
- Workload Identity bindings
- CMEK with envelope encryption
- Private Service Connect and VPC Service Controls
- Cloud IDS and Cloud Logging
- CI gates with synthetic tenants and rollback

## Code Example

```bash
# Illustrative: create a per-tenant CMEK key
gcloud kms keys create tenantA-key --location us-central1 --keyring tenant-ring --purpose encryption

# Illustrative: configure per-tenant Workload Identity
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding tenantA-sa@$PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com \
  --role roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser \
  --member "serviceAccount:$PROJECT.svc.id.goog[tenantA-namespace/tenantA-sa]"

# Illustrative: apply per-tenant Pod Security Standard
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: tenantA-namespace
  labels:
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted
EOF
```

## Why This Is Asked
Assesses ability to prevent cross-tenant data exfiltration using IAM conditions, VPC boundaries, and automated rollback. It also tests how to validate controls under live traffic.

## Key Concepts
- IAM Conditions and per-tenant bindings
- VPC Service Controls and Private Service Connect
- Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK)
- Data Loss Prevention (DLP) on exports
- Automated rollback and synthetic validation

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudo IAM condition binding for per-tenant export control
const binding = {
  role: 'roles/storage.objectAdmin',
  members: ['serviceAccount:exporter@tenant-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com'],
  condition: {
    title: 'tenant_id_match',
    description: 'export allowed only for tenant_id == resource.tenant_id',
    expression: "resource.name.startsWith('projects/.../tenants/' + resource.tenant_id)"
  }
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test the policy with synthetic tenants? 
- How would you handle migration for existing tenants? 
- What metrics and alerts confirm no leakage?

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted multi-tenant SaaS app using Cloud Run and Cloud SQL PostgreSQL, all tenants share a single project. questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical containment across identity, data-at-rest controls, network segmentation, runtime monitoring, and automated rollback.

## Key Concepts

- Per-tenant IAM Conditions and Workload Identity
- CMEK and envelope encryption per tenant
- PSC and VPC Service Controls for network isolation
- Cloud IDS/Armor for real-time detection
- Automated containment playbooks with synthetic tenants for validation

## Code Example

```bash
# Example containment steps (illustrative)
# 1) Revoke tenant credentials
gcloud iam service-accounts keys delete <key-id> --iam-account=<tenant-sa>@<proj>.iam.gserviceaccount.com
# 2) Pause Cloud Run revisions for API gateway
gcloud run revisions list --service <gateway-service>
gcloud run revisions delete <revision-name>
# 3) Enable CMEK per tenant (data at rest)
gcloud kms keys create <tenant-key> --location <location> --keyring <ring> --purpose encryption
```

## Follow-up Questions

- What are edge cases where rollback could fail and how would you mitigate them?
- How would you audit cross-tenant access after containment to ensure no persistence of abuse?

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted SaaS with per-tenant data paths across Cloud Storage, BigQuery, and Pub/Sub, design an automated, tenant-aware data retention and deletion workflow that guarantees per-tenant erasure within regulatory timelines while preserving audit integrity. questions.question: In a two-project GCP data platform used by Plaid and Databricks, a tenant dataset is mistakenly exposed publicly after a misconfiguration. Design an automated containment and remediation workflow that detects per-tenant exposure within 5 minutes, quarantines affected datasets, revokes public access, rotates CMEK keys, and validates data isolation with synthetic tenants. Include exact IAM bindings with conditions, ACM perimeters, and a rollback plan with backups?
questions.answer: Detect public exposure within 5 minutes using Cloud Asset Inventory API exports with scheduled queries and IAM Deny policies; quarantine affected datasets by applying Access Context Manager service perimeters at the tenant level and using dataset-level IAM Conditions that explicitly strip PUBLIC principals; revoke all public access bindings and replace with role-based conditions tied to validated tenant identity; rotate CMEK keys for affected datasets via Cloud KMS key rotation with new key versions and updated key rings; validate data isolation by executing queries with synthetic tenant service accounts to confirm cross-tenant denials and audit all access attempts.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates your expertise in automated incident containment, cross-project IAM controls, and multi-tenant data lake protection in GCP. It tests understanding of timely detection, precise remediation, and auditable rollback procedures.

## Key Concepts

- **Cloud Asset Inventory**: Enables near real-time monitoring of IAM policy changes across all projects
- **IAM Deny Policies**: Provides deny rules that override allow bindings for specific principals or conditions
- **Access Context Manager (ACM) Perimeters**: Enforces service-level access controls across projects and resources
- **BigQuery/Cloud Storage Per-Tenant Isolation**: Dataset-level IAM conditions and VPC Service Controls
- **CMEK Key Rotation**: Automated rotation of Customer-Managed Encryption Keys with version management
- **Synthetic Tenant Validation**: Testing methodology using mock tenant identities to verify isolation

## Remediation Workflow

1. **Detection (0-5 min)**: Cloud Asset Inventory feed combined with Security Command Center findings
2. **Containment (5-15 min)**: Apply ACM perimeter, strip public IAM bindings, enable deny policies
3. **Remediation (15-45 min)**: Rotate CMEK keys, re-provision with tenant-specific conditions
4. **Validation (45-60 min)**: Execute synthetic tenant queries, verify isolation, audit logs

## Exact IAM Binding with Conditions

```yaml
bindings:
- role: roles/bigquery.dataViewer
  members:
  - serviceAccount:tenant-${TENANT_ID}@${PROJECT}.iam.gserviceaccount.com
  condition:
    title: tenant_isolation
    expression: resource.name == "projects/${PROJECT}/datasets/${TENANT_DATASET}" &&
                ip NOT IN client_ips
```

## Rollback Plan

- Maintain automated BigQuery dataset exports to Cloud Storage with retention
- Version IAM policy changes in Cloud Asset Inventory history
- Store CMEK key versions with 30-day recovery window
- Document all changes in Security Command Center findings

questions.question: Design a secure, auditable cross‑organization data sharing pipeline in GCP: a data lake (Cloud Storage + BigQuery) holds PII-derived features; an external analytics partner connects via Private Service Connect to a synthetic dataset exposed for ad-hoc analysis while no raw PII leaves; specify ACM IAM Conditions per-tenant, PSC endpoints in a Shared VPC, CMEK for both stores, DLP masking prior to export, and automated revocation tests with synthetic data and rollback steps?
questions.answer: Implement a comprehensive GCP cross-organization data sharing pipeline with ACM IAM Conditions, PSC endpoints, and DLP masking. Use ACM with per-tenant IAM Conditions to allow BigQuery read-only access to synthetic tables only. Connect partners via Private Service Connect in a Shared VPC architecture. Enforce CMEK for both Cloud Storage and BigQuery. Apply DLP masking before any data export. Implement automated revocation tests with synthetic data and rollback procedures.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical mastery of cross-org data sharing in GCP using ACM, IAM Conditions, PSC, DLP, and lifecycle controls like revocation and rollback.

## Key Concepts
- Access Context Manager (ACM) and IAM Conditions for fine-grained access control
- Private Service Connect (PSC) and Shared VPC for secure connectivity
- Data Loss Prevention (DLP) masking for PII protection
- Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) for data encryption
- Audit logging, revocation tests, and rollback procedures

## Implementation Architecture

**1. ACM IAM Conditions Policy**
```json
{
  "bindings": [
    {
      "role": "roles/bigquery.dataViewer",
      "members": [
        "serviceAccount:partner-sa@org.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
      ],
      "condition": {
        "title": "SyntheticDataOnly",
        "description": "Allow access to synthetic datasets only",
        "expression": "resource.name.startsWith('projects/PROJECT/datasets/synthetic') && request.time <= timestamp('2025-12-31T23:59:59Z')"
      }
    },
    {
      "role": "roles/bigquery.jobUser",
      "members": [
        "serviceAccount:partner-sa@org.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
      ],
      "condition": {
        "title": "SyntheticJobAccess",
        "expression": "resource.name.startsWith('projects/PROJECT/datasets/synthetic')"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**2. PSC and Shared VPC Configuration**
```yaml
# Terraform for PSC endpoint
resource "google_compute_forwarding_rule" "partner_psc" {
  name        = "partner-psc-endpoint"
  target      = google_compute_service_attachment.partner_sa.id
  region      = "us-central1"
  ip_address  = google_compute_address.psc_ip.address
  network     = "projects/host-project/global/networks/shared-vpc"
}

resource "google_compute_service_attachment" "partner_sa" {
  name        = "partner-service-attachment"
  region      = "us-central1"
  connection_preference = "ACCEPT_MANUAL"
 _nat_subnets = [google_compute_subnetwork.psc_subnet.id]
}
```

**3. CMEK Implementation**
```bash
# Create CMEK key ring and keys
gcloud kms keyrings create data-sharing-keys \
  --location=us-central1 \
  --project=host-project

gcloud kms keys create bq-cmek-key \
  --location=us-central1 \
  --keyring=data-sharing-keys \
  --purpose=encryption \
  --rotation-period=90d

# Configure BigQuery with CMEK
bq mk --default_kms_key_name=projects/host-project/locations/us-central1/keyRings/data-sharing-keys/cryptoKeys/bq-cmek-key synthetic_dataset
```

**4. DLP Masking and Synthetic Data Generation**
```python
from google.cloud import dlp_v2
from google.cloud import bigquery

def apply_dlp_masking(project_id, table_id):
    dlp_client = dlp_v2.DlpServiceClient()
    
    # Configure de-identification template
    deidentify_config = {
        "info_type_transformations": {
            "transformations": [
                {
                    "info_types": [{"name": "PERSON_NAME"}, {"name": "EMAIL_ADDRESS"}],
                    "primitive_transformation": {
                        "replace_with_info_type_config": {}
                    }
                },
                {
                    "info_types": [{"name": "PHONE_NUMBER"}],
                    "primitive_transformation": {
                        "character_mask_config": {
                            "masking_character": "*",
                            "number_to_mask": 4
                        }
                    }
                }
            ]
        }
    }
    
    # Apply to BigQuery table
    response = dlp_client.deidentify_content(
        request={"parent": f"projects/{project_id}", "deidentify_config": deidentify_config}
    )
    return response

def generate_synthetic_data(client_config):
    # Generate synthetic data preserving statistical properties
    synthetic_features = {
        "customer_segment": np.random.choice(["premium", "standard", "basic"], p=[0.2, 0.5, 0.3]),
        "risk_score": np.random.normal(0.65, 0.15, 1000),
        "engagement_level": np.random.poisson(3, 1000)
    }
    return synthetic_features
```

**5. Automated Revocation Testing**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# revoke_access_test.sh

# Test 1: IAM Condition validation
echo "Testing IAM Conditions..."
gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --flatten="bindings[].members" \
  --format="table(bindings.role, bindings.members, bindings.condition.title)"

# Test 2: PSC connectivity verification
echo "Testing PSC connectivity..."
gcloud compute forwarding-rules describe partner-psc-endpoint \
  --region=us-central1 --format="value(pscConnectionStatus)"

# Test 3: CMEK key status verification
echo "Testing CMEK key availability..."
gcloud kms keys describe bq-cmek-key \
  --location=us-central1 --keyring=data-sharing-keys \
  --format="value(primary.state)"

# Test 4: DLP masking validation
echo "Testing DLP masking effectiveness..."
bq query --use_legacy_sql=false "
SELECT 
  COUNTIF(REGEXP_CONTAINS(email, r'@')) as email_count,
  COUNTIF(REGEXP_CONTAINS(phone, r'\d{3}-\d{3}-\d{4}')) as phone_count
FROM synthetic_dataset.synthetic_features
WHERE email_count = 0 AND phone_count = 0
"

# Rollback procedure if any test fails
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "Revocation test failed, initiating rollback..."
  gcloud projects remove-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID \
    --member="serviceAccount:partner-sa@org.iam.gserviceaccount.com" \
    --role="roles/bigquery.dataViewer"
  exit 1
fi
```

**6. Monitoring and Audit Logging**
```yaml
# Cloud Logging sink for audit trail
resource "google_logging_sink" "data_sharing_audit" {
  name        = "data-sharing-audit-sink"
  destination = "bigquery.googleapis.com/projects/PROJECT/datasets/audit_logs"
  filter      = "resource.type=bigquery_resource AND protoPayload.methodName=~jobs.*"
}

# Audit query example
SELECT 
  protopayload_auditlog.authenticationInfo.principalEmail,
  protopayload_auditlog.resourceName,
  protopayload_auditlog.methodName,
  timestamp
FROM audit_logs.cloudaudit_googleapis_com_data_access
WHERE timestamp >= TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 24 HOUR)
  AND protopayload_auditlog.resourceName CONTAINS 'synthetic'
```

This implementation ensures zero PII exposure, fine-grained access control, automated revocation capabilities, and comprehensive audit trails for cross-organization data sharing.

  D --> E[Audit logs & revocation tests]

Include: 1) workload identity bindings tying GKE service accounts to Google IAM with least privilege, 2) per-namespace RBAC and IAM policies, 3) network egress controls using Private Service Connect and VPC Service Controls, 4) data protection with CMEK-per-tenant keys and Secret Manager access boundaries, 5) runtime policy enforcement with OPA Gatekeeper/Kyverno and synthetic tenant tests plus a rollback plan?
questions.answer:
Design an automated runtime policy to prevent cross-namespace data exfiltration and privilege escalation in a GCP-hosted multi-tenant SaaS on a shared GKE cluster. The policy must include:

1) Workload Identity bindings tying GKE service accounts to Google IAM with least privilege
2) Per-namespace RBAC and IAM policies

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests the ability to architect runtime, policy-driven security for multi-tenant GKE on GCP, combining IAM, Kubernetes RBAC, networking boundaries, and data protection with testing and rollback capabilities.

## Key Concepts

- **Workload Identity and least-privilege IAM bindings**: Bind Kubernetes service accounts to Google IAM service accounts with minimal required permissions per tenant
- **Namespace-scoped RBAC**: Implement Kubernetes RBAC policies that restrict access to resources within tenant namespaces and prevent cross-namespace access
- **Egress controls**: Use Private Service Connect and VPC Service Controls to enforce network boundaries and prevent data exfiltration
- **Data protection**: Implement CMEK (Customer-Managed Encryption Keys) per tenant and set Secret Manager access boundaries
- **Policy as code**: Use OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno to enforce declarative policies
- **Testing and rollback**: Deploy synthetic tenant workloads to validate controls and maintain rollback procedures

## Code Example

```rego
package kubernetes.admission

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Pod"
  input.request.namespace != ""
  not startswith(input.request.namespace, "tenant-")
  msg := "Pods must be created in tenant-specific namespaces"
}

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Service"
  input.request.operation == "CREATE"
  input.request.object.spec.type == "LoadBalancer"
  input.request.namespace == data.restrict_loadbalancers[_]
  msg := "LoadBalancer services are not allowed in restricted namespaces"
}

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Secret"
  input.request.operation == "CREATE"
  not input.request.object.metadata.namespace
  msg := "Cluster-scoped secrets are prohibited; use namespace-scoped secrets"
}

# Network policies
deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "NetworkPolicy"
  input.request.operation == "CREATE"
  input.request.object.spec.egress == []
  msg := "Egress must be explicitly defined in NetworkPolicy"
}
```

## Rollback Plan

1. **Automated rollback**: Implement GitOps-based rollback using ArgoCD or Flux with versioned policy repositories
2. **Canary testing**: Deploy policy changes to a synthetic tenant first, monitor for 24-48 hours
3. **Quick revert**: Maintain previous policy versions in Git; revert takes effect within 5 minutes via webhook reconfiguration
4. **Alerting**: Set up alerts for policy violations and denied requests to catch issues early

questions.question: New tenant isolation at API gateway: You manage a beginner-level GCP security role for a SaaS app on Cloud Run, single project, multiple tenants. Propose a concrete, implementable plan to enforce per-tenant isolation at the gateway using per-tenant API keys and IAM Conditions, plus CI tests and rollback steps. Include 1) how to issue keys and bind them to tenants, 2) how to enforce tenant constraints at the gateway, 3) how to test isolation in CI, 4) rollback mechanism if misconfiguration detected?
questions.answer: Use Cloud Endpoints ESPv2 at the gateway. Create a per-tenant API key and bind it to a tenant-specific service account; attach IAM Conditions so only a request with that key and the appropriate tenant header (e.g., X-Tenant-ID) can access that tenant's resources. Store each tenant's API key in Secret Manager, and configure the ESPv2 to validate both the API key and the IAM Condition before allowing requests through to Cloud Run.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates practical, beginner-friendly implementation of tenant isolation at the API gateway layer in GCP, using real services and guardrails, plus CI validation and rollback.

## Key Concepts

- **Cloud Endpoints ESPv2**: Acts as the API gateway in front of Cloud Run services, handling authentication and authorization
- **Per-tenant API keys**: Unique keys issued to each tenant for identification and access control
- **IAM Conditions**: Fine-grained access controls that restrict access based on request attributes (e.g., headers)
- **Secret Manager**: Secure storage for tenant API keys
- **Tenant headers**: Custom headers (X-Tenant-ID) that identify which tenant a request belongs to
- **CI testing for access boundaries**: Automated tests to verify tenant isolation is enforced
- **Rollback strategies**: Procedures to quickly disable misconfigured keys or policies

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Issuing Keys and Binding to Tenants
- Generate a unique API key per tenant via GCP Console or gcloud CLI
- Create a dedicated service account for each tenant (e.g., tenant-a-sa@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com)
- Bind the API key to the service account using IAM Conditions:
  ```
  resource.type="cloudrun.googleapis.com/Service" &&
  resource.labels.service="my-saas-app" &&
  apiKeyRequestedCell.value=="[TENANT_KEY_ID]"
  ```
- Store keys securely in Secret Manager with tenant-specific secrets

### 2. Enforcing Tenant Constraints at Gateway
- Configure ESPv2 with OpenAPI spec defining security requirements
- Add validation to check both API key and X-Tenant-ID header
- Use IAM Conditions to ensure the key matches the tenant header
- Implement deny-by-default: any request without valid key+header combination is rejected

### 3. CI Testing for Isolation
- Write integration tests that attempt cross-tenant access (should fail)
- Test valid tenant access (should succeed)
- Test with invalid/missing keys (should fail)
- Use Cloud Build or GitHub Actions to run tests on every deployment

### 4. Rollback Mechanism
- Store previous valid API key versions in Secret Manager
- Use IAM policy versions to rollback to previous policy state
- Implement a kill-switch in the gateway to disable all tenant keys instantly
- Monitor for anomalies and alert on unexpected access patterns

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted data science platform combining Vertex AI Workbench notebooks and Dataflow jobs, tenants share a single project with common Cloud Storage and BigQuery resources. Design a defense-in-depth plan to prevent data leakage and lateral movement if a tenant notebook is compromised. Include: per-tenant IAM Conditions on Vertex AI endpoints, per-tenant Workload Identity and dedicated service accounts, per-tenant CMEK for storage and datasets, per-tenant Private Service Connect to data services, data masking or row-level security strategies, runtime monitoring with Cloud IDS and Cloud Logging, and a CI gate that provisions synthetic tenants and validates cross-tenant isolation with rollback?
questions.answer:
## Defense-in-Depth Multi-Tenant Security Architecture for GCP Data Science Platform

### 1. Per-Tenant IAM Conditions on Vertex AI Endpoints
Configure IAM policies with conditions that restrict notebook access to tenant-specific resources:
```yaml
bindings:
- role: roles/notebooks.runner
  members:
  - user:tenant-a-user@org.com
  condition:
    expression: "resource.name.startsWith('projects/locations/us-central1/instances/tenant-a-')"
```

### 2. Per-Tenant Workload Identity and Dedicated Service Accounts
Each tenant gets a dedicated service account (e.g., `tenant-sa-@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com`) mapped via Workload Identity pool. Notebooks use `google.auth default()` to authenticate—no service account keys stored in notebooks.

### 3. Per-Tenant CMEK for Storage and Datasets
- Cloud Storage: Each tenant has a dedicated KMS key ring and crypto key. Buckets use `kmsKeyName` parameter.
- BigQuery: Datasets use `defaultEncryptionConfiguration.kmsKeyName` pointing to tenant-specific Cloud KMS keys.

### 4. Per-Tenant Private Service Connect
Deploy per-tenant PSC endpoints for BigQuery (`bigquery.googleapis.com`) and Cloud Storage (`storage.googleapis.com`). Tenants access via internal IPs—no public internet exposure.

### 5. Data Masking and Row-Level Security
- BigQuery: Use authorized views with RLS policies (`CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY`)
- Cloud DLP: Apply de-identification templates to mask PII (SSN, email) in query results

### 6. Runtime Monitoring
- Cloud IDS: Deploy regional IDS endpoint for network threat detection
- Cloud Logging: Enable Data Access logs (`DATA_READ`, `DATA_WRITE`) with log sinks to tenant-specific Cloud Storage buckets

### 7. CI Gate with Synthetic Tenant Validation
Terraform provisions isolated test tenants. The pipeline:
1. Creates synthetic tenants with `terraform apply`
2. Runs Go/Python test scripts attempting cross-tenant data access
3. Validates 403 Forbidden errors on unauthorized access
4. On failure: `terraform destroy` rolls back changes and alerts via Cloud Build notifications

## Why This Question Matters
This tests advanced GCP multi-tenant security skills, specifically securing Vertex AI Workbench (the replacement for deprecated managed notebooks). It validates understanding of defense-in-depth: layering identity, encryption, network, and monitoring controls together.

## Core Security Layers

**Identity & Access**
- IAM Conditions use CEL expressions to enforce tenant-specific access rules on Vertex AI endpoints
- Workload Identity lets tenant notebooks authenticate to Google Cloud services without storing service account keys

**Encryption**
- CMEK (Customer-Managed Encryption Keys) gives each tenant control over their encryption keys stored in Cloud KMS
- Even if someone accesses the storage layer, they cannot read tenant data without the tenant's CMEK key

**Network**
- Private Service Connect provides dedicated endpoints for each tenant to access BigQuery and Cloud Storage without traversing the public internet
- Cloud IDS monitors for network-level threats and exfiltration attempts
- VPC Service Controls add additional perimeter defense

**Data Protection**
- Row-level security ensures tenants can only access their own data rows
- Data masking via Cloud DLP redacts sensitive fields before display
- Separate encryption keys prevent cross-tenant data decryption

**Monitoring & Validation**
- Comprehensive logging enables forensic analysis of any suspected breach
- Automated CI testing validates isolation on every change
- Rollback capability ensures rapid recovery from configuration errors
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Matters
This tests advanced GCP multi-tenant security skills, specifically securing Vertex AI Workbench (the replacement for deprecated managed notebooks). It validates understanding of defense-in-depth: layering identity, encryption, network, and monitoring controls together.

## Core Security Layers

**Identity & Access**
- IAM Conditions use CEL expressions to enforce tenant-specific access rules on Vertex AI endpoints
- Workload Identity lets tenant notebooks authenticate to Google Cloud services without storing service account keys

**Encryption**
- CMEK (Customer-Managed Encryption Keys) gives each tenant control over their encryption keys stored in Cloud KMS
- Even if someone accesses the storage layer, they cannot read tenant data without the tenant's specific CMEK key

**Network**
- Private Service Connect provides dedicated endpoints for each tenant to access BigQuery and Cloud Storage without traversing the public internet
- Cloud IDS monitors for network-level threats and exfiltration attempts
- VPC Service Controls add additional perimeter defense around data services

**Data Protection**
- Row-level security in BigQuery ensures tenants can only access their own data rows
- Cloud DLP applies data masking to redact sensitive fields (PII, PHI) before results are displayed
- Separate CMEK keys ensure even storage-level access doesn't enable cross-tenant data decryption

**Monitoring & Validation**
- Cloud Audit Logs with Data Access logs provide comprehensive audit trails
- Cloud IDS detects network anomalies and potential lateral movement
- Automated CI validation with synthetic tenants continuously tests isolation guarantees
- Rollback mechanisms in CI ensure any misconfiguration is immediately reversed

## Key Implementation Details

### IAM Condition Syntax (Corrected)
```javascript
// Correct CEL expression format for Vertex AI resources
resource.name.startsWith('projects/PROJECT/locations/LOCATION/instances/tenant-') &&
resource.type == 'notebooks.googleapis.com/Instance'
```

### Workload Identity Configuration
```hcl
module "tenant-workload-identity" {
  source  = "google-github-actions/sa-credentials/google"
  version = "~> 1.0"
  
  workload_identity_provider = "projects/123456789/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/tenant-pool/providers/tenant-provider"
  service_account_email     = "tenant-sa@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
}
```

### CMEK per Tenant
Each tenant provisions their own Cloud KMS KeyRing and CryptoKey. Storage buckets and BigQuery datasets reference these keys, ensuring tenant data is cryptographically isolated.

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted multi-region SaaS running on GKE with Cloud Run and Cloud SQL, design an mTLS-based service mesh using Private CA for per-tenant mutual authentication. Outline certificate hierarchy and per-tenant issuance/rotation, revocation, tenant-scoped CAs, monitoring, and alerting; include CI validation and rollback if a CA is compromised?
questions.answer: Design a hierarchical Private CA structure with a per-tenant root CA hosted in Cloud HSM, under which intermediate CAs are issued for each tenant cluster. Generate short-lived leaf certificates (24-72 hour validity) bound to SPIFFE IDs for each service workload. Enforce mTLS across GKE and Cloud Run using Istio or Anthos Service Mesh, with Workload Identity binding each service to its tenant-scoped CA. Implement automated certificate rotation via cert-manager or a similar issuance pipeline. Maintain tenant-specific Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) published to Cloud Storage with short revocation windows (1-4 hours). Monitor certificate expiration, issuance rates, and revocation status through Cloud Monitoring, with alerts configured for anomalous issuance patterns or near-expiry certificates. Integrate CI validation to verify certificates include tenant identifiers in Subject Alternative Names (SANs) and validate certificate chains before deployment. In the event of CA compromise, trigger an automated rollback by revoking the compromised intermediate CA, issuing a new CA, rotating all affected leaf certificates, and updating mesh configurations.

questions.question: In a GCP SaaS on Cloud Run, tenants share a single project and a single Cloud Storage bucket stores all documents with object keys prefixed by tenant_id. Draft a beginner-friendly plan to guarantee per-tenant data isolation at runtime. Include: 1) bucket/object-level access controls using IAM and conditional bindings, 2) decision guidance on per-tenant buckets vs. shared bucket with path-based restrictions, 3) auditing and alerts for cross-tenant access attempts, 4) a CI gate that provisions synthetic tenants, validates isolation by attempting cross-tenant access, and rolls back misconfig?
questions.answer:
## Per-Tenant Data Isolation Plan for GCP SaaS on Cloud Run

### 1) Bucket/Object-Level Access Controls Using IAM and Conditional Bindings

Enforce tenant isolation at runtime by leveraging IAM with conditions:

- **Service Account Scoping**: Each tenant's workload (Cloud Run service) uses a dedicated service account.
- **IAM Conditions**: Attach conditions to IAM bindings that verify the tenant context from the JWT token matches the object key prefix.
- **Example Policy**:
```json
{
  "bindings": [{
    "role": "roles/storage.objectViewer",
    "members": ["serviceAccount:tenant-a@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"],
    "condition": {
      "title": "Restrict to tenant-a prefix",
      "expression": "resource.name.startsWith(\"projects/_/buckets/my-bucket/objects/tenant-a/\")"
    }
  }]
}
```

### 2) Per-Tenant Buckets vs. Shared Bucket: Decision Guidance

| Approach | Use When |
|----------|----------|
| **Shared bucket with path-based restrictions** | Tenants are trusted; you need simpler billing and management; compliance allows shared infrastructure |
| **Per-tenant buckets** | Strict regulatory requirements (e.g., HIPAA, GDPR); tenants demand hard isolation; audit requirements mandate complete separation |

**Recommendation**: Start with a shared bucket + IAM conditions for operational simplicity. Migrate to per-tenant buckets only when customer contracts or compliance requirements demand it.

### 3) Auditing and Alerts for Cross-Tenant Access Attempts

- **Cloud Audit Logs**: Enable `DATA_READ` and `DATA_WRITE` logs on the bucket. Create filters to flag operations where the principal does not match the object's tenant prefix.
- **Cloud Monitoring**: Create log-based metrics for denied access (`storage.googleapis.com/authz_denial_count`). Set alerting policies to notify on anomalies.
- **Cloud Asset Inventory**: Query for permission changes that may widen access unexpectedly.

### 4) CI Gate: Synthetic Tenants and Isolation Validation

```yaml
stages:
  - provision_synthetic_tenants:
      - create tenant "synthetic-a"
      - create tenant "synthetic-b"
  - validate_isolation:
      - as synthetic-a, attempt read object "synthetic-b/confidential.pdf"
      - expect: PERMISSION_DENIED
      - as synthetic-a, read object "synthetic-a/document.pdf"
      - expect: SUCCESS
  - rollback_on_failure:
      - if isolation test fails: delete synthetic tenants, fail build, alert team
```

**Key validation points**:
- Negative test: Cross-tenant access must fail
- Positive test: Same-tenant access must succeed
- Automated rollback ensures misconfigurations never reach production

questions.question: In a GCP analytics SaaS, tenants share one GCP project. Data resides in a single BigQuery dataset and a single GCS bucket. Propose a beginner-friendly plan to enforce per-tenant runtime isolation for analytics workloads without duplicating data. Include: 1) BigQuery isolation approach (ROW-LEVEL SECURITY vs AUTHORIZED VIEWS) with justification; 2) data structure naming and access bindings to prevent cross-tenant queries; 3) storage and network controls (IAM bindings, encryption, Private Service Connect or VPC Service Controls); 4) a CI gate that provisions synthetic tenants, runs cross-tenant isolation tests, and safely rolls back misconfig. Also provide a concrete SQL snippet and an IaC example?
questions.answer: Implement per-tenant isolation in BigQuery using Row-Level Security (RLS) on a shared dataset. RLS is preferred over Authorized Views because it enforces filtering at query execution time, requires no separate view objects, and scales better as tenants grow. Create a mapping table that associates IAM service account emails with tenant IDs, then apply ROW ACCESS POLICIES that automatically filter results based on the caller's identity.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question evaluates practical, hands-on knowledge of enforcing runtime data isolation in a shared GCP project using BigQuery features, IAM, and CI/CD.

## Key Concepts
- **BigQuery Row-Level Security (RLS) vs Authorized Views**: RLS filters rows at query execution without separate view objects;Authorized Views require duplicating data logic and managing multiple view objects.
- **Identity-to-Tenant Mapping**: Associate IAM service accounts with tenant context via mapping tables.
- **Storage Boundaries**: GCS bucket policies, Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK), and VPC Service Controls for defense in depth.
- **CI Gates**: Synthetic tenant provisioning with automated rollback for misconfiguration detection.

## Recommended Architecture

### 1. BigQuery Isolation: RLS Approach

**Justification for RLS over Authorized Views:**
- Simpler management at scale without per-tenant view objects
- Transparent to end users; queries automatically filter results
- Better performance for ad-hoc analytics workloads
- Easier to audit and maintain

### 2. Data Structure & Access Bindings

```sql
-- Tenant mapping table associates IAM identities with tenant IDs
CREATE TABLE `proj.ds.user_tenant_map` (
  user_email STRING,
  tenant_id STRING,
  PRIMARY KEY (user_email)
);

-- ROW ACCESS POLICY enforces tenant isolation at query time
CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY tenant_isolation ON `proj.ds.events`
USING (tenant_id IN (
  SELECT tenant_id FROM `proj.ds.user_tenant_map` 
  WHERE user_email = SESSION_USER()
));
```

### 3. Storage & Network Controls

```hcl
# GCS Bucket with uniform bucket-level access
resource "google_storage_bucket" "tenant_data" {
  name          = "shared-tenant-bucket"
  location      = "US"
  uniform_bucket_level_access = true
}

# BigQuery Dataset with IAM
resource "google_bigquery_dataset" "tenant_dataset" {
  dataset_id    = "shared_analytics"
  location      = "US"
}

# IAM Binding with conditions for tenant-scoped access
resource "google_bigquery_dataset_iam_member" "tenant_access" {
  dataset_id = google_bigquery_dataset.tenant_dataset.dataset_id
  role       = "roles/bigquery.dataViewer"
  member     = "serviceAccount:tenant@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
  condition {
    title       = "tenant_scope"
    expression  = "resource.name == 'projects/proj/datasets/shared_analytics'"
  }
}

# VPC Service Controls perimeter
resource "google_access_context_manager_service_perimeter" "tenant_perimeter" {
  parent         = "accessPolicies/12345678"
  title          = "Tenant Data Perimeter"
  status {
    access_levels       = [google_access_context_manager_access_level.tenant_level.name]
    resources           = ["//bigquery.googleapis.com/projects/proj/datasets/shared_analytics"]
    restricted_services = ["bigquery.googleapis.com"]
  }
}

# Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK)
resource "google_kms_crypto_key" "tenant_key" {
  name         = "tenant-cmek"
  key_ring     = google_kms_key_ring.tenant_ring.id
}
```

### 4. CI Gate with Synthetic Tenants

```yaml
# CI Pipeline (GitHub Actions)
name: Tenant Isolation Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  isolation-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Provision synthetic tenants
        run: |
          # Create synthetic tenant in mapping table
          bq query --use_legacy_sql=false \
            "INSERT INTO proj.ds.user_tenant_map (user_email, tenant_id) VALUES ('synthetic_test@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com', 'synthetic_tenant')"
      
      - name: Run cross-tenant isolation tests
        run: |
          # Verify tenant A cannot see tenant B data
          bq query --use_legacy_sql=false \
            --format=csv \
            "SELECT COUNT(*) as row_count FROM proj.ds.events" \
            | grep -v "row_count" | grep -q "^0$" || (echo "ISOLATION FAILURE" && exit 1)
      
      - name: Rollback on failure
        if: failure()
        run: |
          bq query --use_legacy_sql=false \
            "DELETE FROM proj.ds.user_tenant_map WHERE tenant_id = 'synthetic_tenant'"
          echo "Rolled back synthetic tenant"
```

## Summary
Row-Level Security provides transparent, scalable isolation without data duplication. Combined with IAM conditions, VPC Service Controls, and CMEK, this achieves defense-in-depth for multi-tenant analytics workloads in GCP.

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted multi-tenant SaaS app on Cloud Run with a single GCP project, design a beginner-friendly plan for per-tenant API isolation using per-tenant API keys and API Gateway. Include: 1) how keys are bound to tenants and enforced by IAM Conditions, 2) how to prevent cross-tenant access at the gateway and in downstream services, 3) per-tenant quotas and logging, 4) a CI gate that provisions synthetic tenants, validates key scoping, and rolls back misconfig?
questions.answer:
Design a per-tenant API isolation plan using API Gateway and API keys with IAM Conditions. Bind each tenant's API key to their tenant ID using IAM Conditions on the API key resource, restricting access to specific methods or paths. At the gateway, validate that the incoming X-Tenant-ID header matches the tenant ID bound to the API key—if they mismatch, reject the request. Propagate the validated tenant context to downstream services via internal headers or JWTs signed by the gateway.

For per-tenant quotas, configure API Gateway quota settings per API key or implement custom quota enforcement in the application layer. Enable Cloud Logging with tenant-scoped log filters to maintain per-tenant audit trails.

The CI pipeline should provision synthetic test tenants, generate scoped API keys, run integration tests that verify cross-tenant access is blocked, validate quota enforcement, and automatically roll back any configuration that allows cross-tenant data access.

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests practical understanding of API boundary security in a multi-tenant GCP setup, focusing on concrete tooling rather than abstractions.

## Key Concepts
- Cloud Endpoints ESPv2 and IAP as the security boundary
- IAM Conditions for per-tenant access control
- Per-tenant service accounts with least-privilege bindings
- Private Service Connect / VPC Service Controls for network isolation
- CI gate with synthetic tenants and rollback strategy

## Code Example
```yaml
# ESPv2 OpenAPI security scheme enabling JWT tenant_id validation
security:
  - tenant: []
securitySchemes:
  tenant:
    type: http
    scheme: bearer
    bearerFormat: JWT
    x-google-jwt-locations:
      - header: Authorization
        prefix: Bearer
      - query: access_token
```

## Implementation Plan
1. **JWT Tenant Validation**: Configure ESPv2 to extract tenant_id from JWT claims and validate against IAM Conditions
2. **Per-Tenant Service Accounts**: Create dedicated service accounts per tenant with bindings scoped to tenant-specific resources
3. **Network Controls**: Deploy Private Service Connect or VPC Service Controls to enforce egress restrictions
4. **CI Gate**: Provision synthetic test tenants, verify isolation by attempting cross-tenant access, and rollback on failure

name: projects/PROJECT_ID/denypolicies/containment

Include: 1) redaction/masking of tenant_id and PII in Cloud Logging and stack traces, 2) per-tenant log sinks and audit trails, 3) IAM/Conditions controls on log access and monitoring, 4) a CI pipeline that validates tenant isolation in logs and rolls back misconfig?
questions.answer: Use structured logging with tenant_id as a dedicated field; implement automated PII and tenant_id masking across all logs, stack traces, and error messages; configure per-tenant log sinks with tenant-scoped filters to maintain isolated audit trails; enforce least-privilege IAM access controls using IAM Conditions to restrict log access by tenant; and implement a CI/CD pipeline that validates tenant isolation in logs before deployment, with automated rollback for misconfigurations.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests practical logging security for multi-tenant GCP applications, focusing on data leakage risks in logs, error traces, and audit trails.

## Key Concepts
- Structured logging with automated PII redaction
- Per-tenant log sinks and audit trail isolation
- IAM Conditions and role-based access control on log data
- CI/CD validation and automated rollback strategies for tenant isolation

## Code Example
```python
import logging
import re

def redact_log(entry: dict) -> dict:
    '''Redact tenant_id and PII from log entries before emission.'''
    redacted = entry.copy()
    redacted['tenant_id'] = 'REDACTED'
    
    pii_fields = ['email', 'phone', 'ssn', 'credit_card']
    for field in pii_fields:
        if field in redacted:
            redacted[field] = 'REDACTED'
    
    if 'message' in redacted:
        redacted['message'] = re.sub(r'\b[\w.-]+@[\w.-]+\.\w+\b', '[EMAIL]', redacted['message'])
    
    return redacted

class TenantAwareLogger:
    def __init__(self, tenant_id: str):
        self.logger = logging.getLogger(f'tenant.{tenant_id}')
        self.tenant_id = tenant_id
    
    def log(self, level: str, message: str, **kwargs):
        entry = {'tenant_id': self.tenant_id, 'message': message, **kwargs}
        redacted = redact_log(entry)
        getattr(self.logger, level.lower())(redacted)
```

## Implementation Notes
1. Use Cloud Logging's built-in exclusion filters to drop sensitive fields before ingestion
2. Configure Log Router sinks with tenant-specific Cloud Storage buckets or BigQuery datasets
3. Apply IAM Conditions on Log Viewer roles: `resource.name.startsWith('projects/_/buckets/tenant-{id}')`
4. In CI pipeline, run log inspection tests that verify no tenant data appears in unrelated log streams

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests practical mastery of tenant isolation at rest, cryptographic key management, and production-grade automation. It requires concrete configuration patterns rather than abstractions.

## Key Concepts

- Per-tenant Cloud KMS keys (CMEK) for data-at-rest protection
- Envelope encryption: per-tenant DEKs wrapped by CMEK
- Regular CMEK rotation and safe backup re-wrapping
- IAM Roles: CryptoKeyEncrypter/Decrypter with per-tenant IAM Conditions
- Metadata linking keys to data assets; auditability and replay prevention
- CI gate to simulate tenants, test encryption/decryption, and validate rollback

## Code Example

```bash
# Create CMEK per tenant
gcloud kms keyrings create tenants --location global
gcloud kms keys create tenantA-datakey --location global --keyring tenants --purpose encryption

# Bind app service to key with per-tenant constraint (example)
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding my-project \
  --member="serviceAccount:tenantA-app@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com" \
  --role="roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter" \
  --condition="expression=resource.name.startsWith('projects/_/locations/global/keyRings/tenants/cryptoKeys/tenantA-datakey', caseSensitive=false),title=TenantAKeyAccess"
```

## Follow-up Questions

- What are the audit logging and incident response implications of per-tenant CMEKs?

questions.question: In a GCP-hosted SaaS on GKE with tenants isolated by namespaces, design an incident-response and containment plan to detect and cut off a compromised tenant namespace within 15 minutes. Include: per-tenant NetworkPolicy and firewall rules, per-tenant Workload Identity with least privilege, per-tenant Secret Manager entries with CMEK, namespace quarantine with pod eviction and credential rotation, Cloud IDS/Logging alerts, and a CI gate that validates isolation before restoring traffic?
questions.answer:
## 15-Minute Incident Response & Containment Plan for Compromised Tenant Namespace

### Detection Phase (0-2 min)
- **Cloud IDS** triggers alert on anomalous traffic patterns (outbound to C2 servers, lateral movement)
- **Cloud Logging** anomaly detection flags suspicious API activity (credential abuse, privilege escalation)
- Automated webhook activates incident playbook

### Containment Phase (2-5 min)
- **NetworkPolicy**: Apply deny-all egress to tenant namespace; block ingress via Cloud Armor
- **Firewall Rules**: Revoke ingress/fgress firewall rules for the tenant's VPC subnetwork
- **Pod Eviction**: Force-delete all pods in the compromised namespace to halt malicious execution

### Credential Isolation (5-8 min)
- **Workload Identity**: Disable the compromised namespace's service account; rotate keys
- **Secret Manager**: Rotate all tenant secrets; re-encrypt with new CMEK version
- **API Keys/OAuth**: Revoke and regenerate all credentials scoped to the namespace

### Quarantine & Monitoring (8-12 min)
- Apply namespace annotation `isolation.lockdown: true`
- Enable enhanced audit logging for all API server requests in namespace
- Route all traffic to a static error page via ingress controller

### CI Gate for Restoration (12-15 min)
- **Automated Validation Pipeline**:
  - Verify NetworkPolicy denies all non-essential traffic
  - Confirm Workload Identity has least-privilege bindings only
  - Validate no stale secrets remain in Secret Manager
  - Test pod sandbox isolation
- Manual security review sign-off required before traffic restoration

- **Namespace quarantine**: Pod eviction and credential rotation to sever attacker access
- **Telemetry**: Cloud IDS for network threat detection, Cloud Logging for audit trails
- **CI gate for safe restoration**: Automated validation ensuring full isolation before reinstating traffic

# Kubernetes NetworkPolicy example (deny by default)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:

  policyTypes:

  - Egress

```javascript
# Istio AuthorizationPolicy example (per-tenant)
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: AuthorizationPolicy
metadata:
  name: tenant-a-api
  namespace: tenant-a
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api
  rules:
  - from:
    - source:
        principals: ["cluster.local/ns/tenant-a/sa/tenant-a-api"]
```

questions.answer:
In a GCP-hosted multi-tenant analytics SaaS that writes all tenants' data to a single BigQuery dataset, design a beginner-friendly plan to guarantee tenant data isolation for queries and dashboards. Include: 1) per-tenant access via authorized views or row-level filters, 2) per-tenant IAM bindings for the dataset, 3) monitoring/logging for cross-tenant access attempts and alerting, 4) a CI gate that provisions synthetic tenants, runs isolation tests on sample queries, and safely rolls back misconfigurations.

Answer: Expose only tenant-specific data via authorized views or row-level filters, grant dataset access to tenant-specific service accounts using the principle of least privilege, and enable audit logging with alerting to detect and respond to any cross-tenant access attempts.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question assesses practical understanding of BigQuery data isolation patterns and how to enforce them using IAM, views, and CI validation in a multi-tenant SaaS environment.

## Key Concepts

- **Authorized views and row-level filters** in BigQuery to restrict data access per tenant
- **Per-tenant IAM bindings** using the principle of least privilege
- **Audit logging and alerting** to detect abnormal or unauthorized access patterns
- **CI/CD validation** with synthetic tenants and rollback mechanisms for misconfigurations

## Code Example

```sql
-- Example per-tenant authorized view
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW tenant_a.orders AS
SELECT * FROM shared.orders
WHERE tenant_id = 'tenant_a';

-- Example row-level security filter
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE shared.orders
ROW ACCESS POLICY tenant_filter
ON shared.orders
USING (tenant_id = SESSION_USER());
```

## Implementation Plan

1. **Data Isolation Layer**: Create separate authorized views for each tenant that filter the underlying shared tables by tenant ID. Alternatively, use row-level security policies that automatically filter data based on the authenticated user's session.

2. **IAM Bindings**: Provision dedicated service accounts per tenant and grant them access only to their specific authorized views—not the underlying dataset. Apply the principle of least privilege throughout.

3. **Monitoring & Alerting**: Enable Data Access audit logs on the dataset and configure Cloud Logging sinks with alerts for any access attempts outside expected tenant boundaries. Set up dashboards to track access patterns.

4. **CI Validation**: Build a pipeline that creates synthetic tenant datasets, runs sample queries to verify isolation (e.g., confirm Tenant A cannot see Tenant B's data), and automatically rolls back to the last known-good configuration upon failure.

questions.answer: Implement a defense-in-depth plan with five layers: 1) Tenant-scoped DB roles and IAM Conditions—create database roles per tenant and bind each tenant's service account to its corresponding role using IAM Conditions with resource tags; 2) PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) policies on tenant_id, enforced at session level with custom search_path to prevent bypass; 3) Network controls using Private Service Connect for private API/database access and VPC Service Controls to define perimeter boundaries preventing data exfiltration; 4) CI gate that provisions synthetic tenants during testing, attempts cross-tenant data access, and automatically rolls back/deploys a block if isolation is breached; 5) Cloud Audit Logs, Data Access logs, and drift detection alerts via Cloud Monitoring to catch misconfigurations in real-time.

## Why This Is Asked

## Key Concepts

- **PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS)**: Policy-based row access control tied to tenant_id column
- **IAM Conditions and resource tagging**: Fine-grained binding of tenant service accounts to specific database roles
- **Private Service Connect (PSC)**: Private IP connectivity to Cloud SQL without exposing data to public internet
- **VPC Service Controls**: Service perimeter boundaries to prevent data exfiltration
- **Synthetic tenants in CI**: Automated regression testing with isolated tenant isolates to validate isolation guarantees
- **Audit logging and drift alerting**: Cloud Audit Logs, Data Access logs, and Cloud Monitoring alerts for configuration changes

## Code Example

```javascript
// Example RLS policy for PostgreSQL
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation_policy ON orders
  USING (current_setting('app.tenant_id') = tenant_id);

// Set tenant context per session
ALTER ROLE app_user SET app.tenant_id = 'tenant-123';
```

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests the ability to design per-tenant data isolation in a shared BigQuery dataset, combining RLS, CMEK, and perimeter controls. It evaluates understanding of trade-offs between performance and security, plus awareness of robust CI/gate strategies for synthetic tenants.

## Key Concepts

- **BigQuery Row-Level Security (RLS)**: Implement column-level and row-level security policies that filter data at query time based on tenant context, eliminating the need for separate datasets.
- **CMEK and Envelope Encryption**: Use customer-managed encryption keys with envelope encryption, where a master key encrypts data keys that in turn encrypt tenant-specific data. This ensures tenant data remains cryptographically isolated.
- **IAM Conditions and Tenant-Scoped Identities**: Leverage IAM Conditions to restrict access based on session attributes, enabling dynamic tenant context without creating separate service accounts.
- **IAP/Cloud Armor**: Protect data consumer applications using Identity-Aware Proxy for authentication, combined with Cloud Armor for rate limiting and DDoS protection.
- **Cloud Audit Logs and Monitoring**: Configure Cloud Audit Logs to capture all BigQuery access attempts with tenant attribution, and set up Cloud Monitoring alerts for anomalous query patterns or unauthorized access attempts.
- **CI Gates and Rollback Strategies**: Implement automated testing pipelines that provision synthetic tenants, execute cross-tenant access validation tests, and automatically rollback configurations if isolation failures are detected.

## Code Example

```sql
-- Create a row-level security policy for tenant isolation
CREATE SECURITY POLICY IF NOT EXISTS tenant_isolation_policy
OPTIONS (
  row_filter = 'tenant_id',
  description = 'Filters rows based on tenant context'
)
AS (
  SELECT tenant_id
  FROM session_context()
  WHERE session_context().tenant_id = tenant_id
);

-- Apply the security policy to sensitive tables
ALTER TABLE shared_dataset.sales_data
ADD POLICY tenant_isolation_policy;

-- Verify policy is applied
SELECT * FROM shared_dataset.sensitive_tables
WHERE _row_access_policy_applied = TRUE;
```

questions.question: In a GCP SaaS app hosted on Cloud Run sharing a single project, with Cloud SQL PostgreSQL and Pub/Sub, draft a beginner-friendly plan to prevent privilege abuse and data leakage. questions.answer: Implement per-endpoint service accounts with least-privilege IAM bindings—using Cloud Run Invoker for API access and Cloud SQL Client scoped specifically to the application. Apply IAM Conditions to further restrict access based on attributes. Enforce tenant_id filtering at the API layer and implement basic Row-Level Security (RLS) policies in the database to guarantee tenant isolation. Use Private IP or Private Service Connect (PSC) for secure internal communication between services. Finally, establish a CI gate that scans IAM bindings, rejects overly broad role grants, and provides a rollback mechanism for misconfigurations.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests practical, beginner-friendly security controls in a real GCP setup, ensuring policies translate to concrete implementation steps.

## Key Concepts

- API-layer tenant isolation with tenant_id controls and basic RLS
- Private networking (Private IP/PSC) for service-to-service communication
- CI/CD gate with rollback for misconfiguration prevention

## Code Example
```yaml
# Example CI check pseudo
- name: Scan IAM bindings
  run: gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format=yaml > policy.yaml
  
- name: Validate least privilege
  run: |
    # Check for overly broad roles
    if grep -q 'roles/owner\|roles/editor' policy.yaml; then
      echo "ERROR: Broad roles detected"
      exit 1
    fi
    # Verify service account usage
    echo "IAM policy validated"

- name: Rollback on failure
  run: gcloud projects set-iam-policy PROJECT_ID policy.yaml.bak
```

questions.question: In a GCP multi-tenant SaaS sharing a single project (Cloud Run, Firestore, Pub/Sub), design an automated policy drift system to enforce tenant-specific least-privilege IAM bindings. How would you detect drift, perform automatic rollback to last-good bindings, and validate with synthetic tenants? Include Cloud Asset Inventory, IAM Conditions, and CI/CD integration?
questions.answer: Establish per-tenant IAM binding baselines stored as versioned templates in a Git repository. Continuously reconcile live bindings using Cloud Asset Inventory's Policy Analyzer against these baselines. On drift detection, automatically trigger a rollback to the last-known-good version stored in the template repository, then validate the rollback using synthetic test tenants to confirm least-privilege enforcement is restored.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates your ability to automate IAM policy governance at scale—a critical concern in single-project multi-tenant architectures. It assesses your understanding of drift detection, safe rollback mechanisms, policy-as-code principles, and CI/CD integration.

## Key Concepts

- IAM bindings and least-privilege principles
- Cloud Asset Inventory and Policy Analyzer
- Policy as code with versioned templates
- IAM Conditions and VPC Service Controls
- Drift detection, automated rollback, and synthetic validation
- CI/CD integration and audit logging

## Architecture Overview

1. **Baseline Storage**: Store per-tenant IAM bindings as versioned YAML/JSON templates in a Git repository, using semantic versioning for rollback tracking.

2. **Drift Detection**: Use Cloud Asset Inventory's Policy Analyzer API to query current bindings and compare them against stored baselines. Schedule this check via Cloud Scheduler or Cloud Run Cron.

3. **Automated Rollback**: On drift detection, retrieve the last-known-good version from the template store and apply it via the IAM API. Log all changes to Cloud Audit Logs.

4. **Synthetic Validation**: Create test tenants with known identities to verify that bindings are correctly applied and least-privilege is enforced after rollback.

5. **CI/CD Integration**: Integrate drift checks into your CI/CD pipeline using Cloud Build or GitHub Actions to block deployments that would violate tenant policies.

questions.question: In a GCP-native real-time feature store shared by three brands, design a per-brand access model for streaming features from Pub/Sub through Dataflow to BigQuery. Define exact IAM bindings and conditions, per-brand VPC Service Controls boundaries, Private Service Connect endpoints for feature API calls, and CMEK strategies; include rotation cadence and a rollback test plan that preserves in-flight data?
questions.answer: Design a comprehensive per-brand access model for a GCP-native real-time feature store shared by three brands. For each brand, create dedicated service accounts with IAM conditions: `resource.name.startsWith('projects/PROJECT/datasets/brand_a_')` and `resource.name.startsWith('projects/PROJECT/subscriptions/brand_a_')`. Implement VPC Service Controls boundaries per brand with egress policies blocking unauthorized data transfers. Deploy Private Service Connect endpoints for feature API calls using brand-specific forwarding rules: `forwardingRule: brand-a-feature-api.endpoints.internal`. Configure CMEK with separate key rings per brand: `brand-a-key-ring` for storage and `brand-a-bq-key-ring` for BigQuery. Rotate keys quarterly using automated Cloud KMS rotation with 7-day overlap. ## Code Example

```javascript
// Example: generate DR test plan commands for a tenant
function drTestPlan(tenantId){
  return [
    `pg_dump -h ${'DB_HOST'} -U ${'DB_USER'} -d ${'DB_NAME'} -t "tenant_${tenantId}.*" > backups/${tenantId}.dump`,
    `node restoreTenant.js --tenant ${tenantId} --target ${'DR_TARGET'}`
  ]
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

questions.answer: Per-tenant isolation via dedicated BigQuery datasets with row-level access policies; per-tenant CMEK for Cloud Storage and BigQuery data; enforce IAM Conditions on API endpoints and Pub/Sub; enable Data Access logs with per-tenant sinks; provision Private Service Connect endpoints with VPC Service Controls per tenant; implement data provenance via Dataflow metadata tracking and BigQuery job labels; CI pipeline provisions synthetic tenants and validates isolation boundaries with automated rollback on failure.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical, end-to-end data governance in a multi-tenant analytics stack, including provenance and rollback capabilities.

## Key Concepts
- BigQuery row-level access control and authorized views for fine-grained data isolation
- Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) per tenant for encryption key separation
- Private networking: Private Service Connect and VPC Service Controls for perimeter security
- IAM Conditions for per-tenant API access enforcement
- Data provenance with Rekor/Cosign for Dataflow and BigQuery pipeline steps
- CI gate for synthetic tenant provisioning with isolation validation and rollback

## Code Example
```terraform
# Per-tenant dataset and access bindings
resource "google_bigquery_dataset" "tenant" {
  for_each = var.tenants
  dataset_id    = "tenant_${each.key}"
  location      = var.region
  default_table_expiration_ms = 3600000
  labels        = { tenant = each.key, environment = var.env }
}

resource "google_bigquery_access" "tenant_binding" {
  for_each = google_bigquery_dataset.tenant
  dataset_id = each.value.dataset_id
  role   = "roles/bigquery.dataViewer"
  user_by_email = each.value.tenant_email
}

resource "google_kms_crypto_key" "tenant_cmek" {
  for_each   = var.tenants
  name       = "tenant-${each.key}-key"
  key_ring   = google_kms_key_ring.tenant[each.key].id
  rotation_period = 8640000
}

resource "google_bigquery_dataset" "tenant_encrypted" {
  for_each = var.tenants
  dataset_id    = "tenant_${each.key}_secure"
  location      = var.region
  default_encryption_configuration {
    kms_key_name = google_kms_crypto_key.tenant_cmek[each.key].id
  }
}
```
